title: Hybrid Dynamical Systems
citation: Rafal Goebel, Ricardo G. Sanfelice, and Andrew R. Teel, ‘Hybrid Dynamical Systems,’ IEEE Control Systems Magazine, 29(2):28–93, 2009
access: coauthor-hosted IEEE reading copy and author publication record
version: IEEE version of record hosted by a coauthor
influence_basis: Recipient of the 2010 IEEE Control Systems Magazine Outstanding Paper Award and an authoritative tutorial on the modern flow-jump framework.
notes: The article describes itself as a tutorial rather than a comprehensive literature survey.

title: Control Barrier Function Based Quadratic Programs for Safety Critical Systems
citation: Aaron D. Ames, Xiangru Xu, Jessy W. Grizzle, and Paulo Tabuada, ‘Control Barrier Function Based Quadratic Programs for Safety Critical Systems,’ IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, 62(8):3861–3876, 2017
access: author manuscript and formal publication metadata in CaltechAUTHORS
version: author manuscript corresponding to the 2017 version of record
influence_basis: Foundational CBF-QP formulation; a 2024 Annual Reviews in Control tutorial explicitly includes this paper among the works through which control barrier functions emerged as a powerful safety-control tool.
notes: Observations use the 16-page author-manuscript numbering supplied by CaltechAUTHORS.

cue: is a subset; is a set-valued mapping
summary: Types the flow and jump sets as state-space subsets and the flow and jump maps as set-valued state mappings before defining a hybrid arc.
Presents a hybrid system through separate constrained flow and jump laws, retaining both the applicable sets and the possibility of set-valued dynamics.

locator: Sidebar “Existence, Uniqueness, and Other Well-Posedness Issues,” pp. 43–44
cue: may not have solutions; may not have unique solutions
summary: Separates classical well-posedness from the adopted hybrid framework by explicitly allowing failure of existence or uniqueness, then treats viability conditions only as sufficient routes to nontrivial solutions.

Under the basic hybrid assumptions and target-set compatibility, uses strict Lyapunov decrease during both flows and jumps as a one-way sufficient condition for pre-asymptotic stability and a qualified basin conclusion.

cue: assume that the set is defined as
summary: Defines a safe set, its boundary, and its interior through the sign of one continuously differentiable scalar function, keeping the three set relations parallel.

locator: §II-C, Propositions 1 and 3 and synthesis paragraph, author manuscript pp. 5–6
summary: Combines the sufficient and converse results to characterize forward invariance by existence of a zeroing barrier function, while retaining compactness, nonemptiness, smoothness, and set-regularity assumptions needed by the converse.

locator: §IV-B, CLF-CBF QP and Eqs. (33)–(34), author manuscript p. 8
summary: States the controller as a quadratic program with control and relaxation variables, a positive-definite quadratic objective, a relaxed Lyapunov constraint, and an unrelaxed barrier constraint representing safety.

cue: may no longer be valid; remains an open question
summary: Limits the higher-relative-degree construction to the unconstrained-input setting and explicitly identifies constrained-input design as unresolved rather than extending the result without support.

title: Model Predictive Control: Theory, Computation, and Design
citation: James B. Rawlings, David Q. Mayne, and Moritz M. Diehl, Model Predictive Control: Theory, Computation, and Design, 2nd ed., 6th printing, Nob Hill Publishing, 2026
license: copyright Nob Hill Publishing, all rights reserved; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Durably maintained comprehensive graduate textbook on MPC, now in the sixth printing of its second edition, with foundational theory, computation, exercises, and official course-adoption materials.
notes: Observations use the official sixth-printing PDF at https://sites.engineering.ucsb.edu/~jbraw/mpc/MPC-book-2nd-edition-6th-printing.pdf and its printed pagination. Free access does not imply an open license.

conditions: The safe set is defined by a control barrier function for the stated control-affine system.
The feedback law is Lipschitz continuous and satisfies the pointwise CBF constraint.
locator: §III-B, Corollary 2 and Remark 9, author manuscript p. 7
cue: then the set is forward invariant
summary: Concludes forward invariance of the safe set from a controller satisfying the barrier constraint, while retaining the barrier-function and feedback-regularity hypotheses.

conditions: The distinction concerns whether the relevant hybrid solutions are complete.
locator: Stability definitions and discussion, pp. 58–59, coauthor-hosted IEEE copy
cue: pre-asymptotically stable; asymptotically stable if, in addition
summary: Distinguishes pre-asymptotic stability from asymptotic stability by the additional completeness requirement instead of silently treating finite-domain attraction as an asymptotic claim.

conditions: The initial state is a parameter of both the finite-horizon cost and feasible control-sequence set.
locator: Ch. 2, §2.2, pp. 95–97, especially Eqs. (2.3)–(2.8) on pp. 96–97, sixth-printing PDF
cue: define the optimal control problem; minimize over
summary: Defines the finite-horizon MPC problem by minimizing the horizon cost over a feasible control sequence, keeping the initial-state parameter, decision sequence, objective, dynamics, and constraints distinct.

conditions: The initial state is feasible for the finite-horizon problem.
locator: Ch. 2, §2.2, Proposition 2.4, pp. 98–99; assumptions on pp. 97–98, sixth-printing PDF
cue: the set of solutions is nonempty; may be set valued
summary: Establishes existence of a finite-horizon minimizer for feasible initial states under compactness and continuity assumptions, while explicitly allowing the optimizer and MPC law to be set-valued.

conditions: The system satisfies the stated IOSS property.
The conclusion is restricted to the theorem's feasible closed-loop set.
locator: Ch. 2, §2.4.4, Assumption 2.23 and Theorem 2.24, pp. 121–123; theorem on p. 122, sixth-printing PDF
cue: is a Lyapunov function; is asymptotically stable in
summary: Uses the stated terminal and detectability hypotheses to obtain a Lyapunov function and asymptotic stability on the specified feasible set, without upgrading the result to unconditional global stability.

cue: optimality alone does not ensure; sufficient conditions
summary: Explains that finite-horizon optimality alone does not provide the Lyapunov decrease needed for closed-loop stability and motivates additional stage-cost, terminal-cost, and terminal-set conditions.

conditions: The nominal recursive-feasibility result uses the invariant terminal-set and standing model assumptions.
locator: Ch. 2, §2.3, Proposition 2.10(b) and concluding discussion, pp. 110–112; uncertainty limitation on p. 112, sixth-printing PDF
cue: recursively feasible; may lose feasibility in the presence of
summary: Scopes recursive feasibility to the nominal closed-loop construction and warns that disturbances, model error, or state-estimation error can destroy it.

intent: State a control-theoretic implication together with the regularity, feasibility, and set restrictions that license it.
constructions: if the controller satisfies ..., then ... is forward invariant
provided that ..., the closed loop is ... on ...
the Lyapunov inequalities are sufficient for ...
within the feasible set, the theorem establishes ...
boundaries: Retain every regularity, feasibility, invariance, detectability, and solution-completeness hypothesis used by the cited result.
Preserve implication direction; a sufficient Lyapunov or barrier condition is not automatically necessary.
Keep local, regional, and feasible-set conclusions distinct from global claims.
Forward invariance of a safe set does not by itself establish stabilization, optimality, or performance.
Do not transfer a nominal closed-loop implication to uncertain dynamics without a robustness result.

boundary_cases.boundary: Keep local, regional, and feasible-set conclusions distinct from global claims.
boundary_cases.challenge: A theorem proving asymptotic stability on the feasible MPC set is summarized as global asymptotic stability.
boundary_cases.safe_response: Name the theorem's feasible set and retain all terminal and detectability assumptions.

boundary_cases.boundary: Forward invariance of a safe set does not by itself establish stabilization, optimality, or performance.
boundary_cases.challenge: A control-barrier-function condition is described as proving both safety and convergence to the origin.
boundary_cases.safe_response: State only forward invariance unless a separate convergence result has been supplied.

synthetic_example: Under the stated terminal-set and detectability assumptions, the value function decreases along the closed loop; hence, the origin is asymptotically stable on the feasible set.

intent: Explain the scope or failure boundary of a control guarantee without turning a nominal, safety, or pre-asymptotic result into a stronger claim.
constructions: in the presence of ..., recursive feasibility may fail
pre-asymptotic stability becomes asymptotic stability when ...
the safety guarantee does not imply ...
boundaries: Finite-horizon optimality does not by itself imply closed-loop stability.
Nominal recursive feasibility does not establish robustness to disturbances, model mismatch, or estimation error.
Distinguish pre-asymptotic stability from asymptotic stability when completeness of solutions is not established.
A safety or invariance result does not automatically provide convergence or performance.
Retain relative-degree, input-constraint, and regularity limits when interpreting a barrier-function construction.

boundary_cases.boundary: Finite-horizon optimality does not by itself imply closed-loop stability.
boundary_cases.challenge: Solving each finite-horizon problem optimally is said to guarantee asymptotic stability without a Lyapunov-decrease argument.
boundary_cases.safe_response: State optimality and stability separately, then name the terminal or decrease assumptions used for stability.

boundary_cases.boundary: Nominal recursive feasibility does not establish robustness to disturbances, model mismatch, or estimation error.
boundary_cases.challenge: A nominal shift argument is extrapolated to perturbed closed-loop trajectories.
boundary_cases.safe_response: Restrict recursive feasibility to the nominal model unless a robust MPC result covers the uncertainty.

boundary_cases.boundary: Distinguish pre-asymptotic stability from asymptotic stability when completeness of solutions is not established.
boundary_cases.challenge: Attraction along all existing hybrid solutions is called asymptotic stability even though some solutions may terminate in finite hybrid time.
boundary_cases.safe_response: Use pre-asymptotic terminology until completeness is part of the result.

synthetic_example: The nominal shift argument yields recursive feasibility, but this conclusion does not cover model mismatch or state-estimation error.
